_amendment_note: Filed before any data collection. No results seen. Corrects three issues identified by external critic.

framing_correction.old: AI identifies biologically important neurons that human attention misses
framing_correction.new: AI ranking approximates lesion-importance distribution more closely than human literature ranking does. Centrality is a proxy for lesion sensitivity, not a direct measure of function.
framing_correction.reason: Hub centrality != functional importance in general. Hubs can be redundant. The correct claim is about ranking alignment, not causal discovery.

null_model.description: N=185 neurons in subgraph, K=10 command interneurons (essential set), n=20 AI-ranked top-K. Under random selection, P(overlap >= k) is computed exactly.
null_model.note: Our pre-registered threshold is k=6 (P=0.000120 under null). This is not tuned to results — it was derived from the hypergeometric distribution before any AI responses were collected.

corrected_H2_pred.statement: AI anon top-20 contains >= 4 of 10 locomotion command interneurons (AVAL, AVAR, AVBL, AVBR, PVCL, PVCR, RIML, RIMR, AVDL, AVDR). Under hypergeometric null (N=185, K=10, n=20), P(X>=4)=0.014, P(X>=5)=0.0016, P(X>=6)=0.00012. Threshold is set at k=4 (p<0.05) for confirmation, k=6 (p<0.001) for strong confirmation.
corrected_H2_pred.ground_truth_source: White_1986 ablation + locomotion circuit literature, defined before experiment

SSPH_framing.name: Structural Salience Projection Hypothesis
SSPH_framing.formulation: Different ranking systems (human literature, AI ranking, lesion impact) induce different projections over the same underlying graph. These projections differ systematically in which graph-theoretic dimension they approximate. OPL is one instance: AI ranking approximates d2 (in-degree) projection while human attention approximates d1 (or non-structural) projection.
SSPH_framing.what_this_experiment_tests: Whether AI ranking projection aligns with lesion-importance projection (White 1986) more than human attention projection does. This is not a claim that AI discovers function — it is a claim about projection alignment across independently defined ranking systems.
